texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 'Membrane reactors' are robust materials that can enable chemical reaction and separation of one species from mixture owing to their designed microscopic structure and properties. Basic sciences of membrane reactors are studied through thin films. They separate a feed of (A + B) into permeate C and reject D streams. Many chemical reactions occur to a limited extent due to equilibrium constraints; in such cases, removal of one of the products from the reaction mixture will drive the reaction in the forward direction (Le-Chatelier's principle), increase efficiency(completion of reaction) and improve economics of the reaction process. Thin films are typically made using techniques such as solution casting and phase inversion, solution casting and heat-drying, LangmuirBlodgett method, electro-static self assembly, covalent molecular assembly, chemical vapour deposition films and conventional casting techniques like die-pressing and spin-coating. In this paper, the
texts.text: 'Membrane reactors' are robust materials that can enable chemical reaction and separation of one species from mixture owing to their designed microscopic structure and properties. Basic sciences of membrane reactors are studied through thin films. They separate a feed of (A + B) into permeate C and reject D streams. Many chemical reactions occur to a limited extent due to equilibrium constraints; in such cases, removal of one of the products from the reaction mixture will drive the reaction in the forward direction (Le-Chatelier's principle), increase efficiency(completion of reaction) and improve economics of the reaction process. Thin films are typically made using techniques such as solution casting and phase inversion, solution casting and heat-drying, LangmuirBlodgett method, electro-static self assembly, covalent molecular assembly, chemical vapour deposition films and conventional casting techniques like die-pressing and spin-coating. In this paper, the

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thin films of oligomers were synthesized on PTFE substrates and they were studied for separation of product from liquid chemical reaction mixture. Three different types of membranes were fabricated using a technique called 'covalent molecular assembly'. Covalent molecular assembly is a synthesis technique in which a substrate is sequentially immersed in chemical solutions to grow molecular moieties with covalent bonding to the underlying layer of molecules on the substrate. The first membrane was oligoimide PMDA-ODA. The second membrane was crosslinked oligoamide TC-TAB. The third membrane was Nickel terminated oligoimide Ni-PMDA-ODA. The first two membranes were studied for Friedel-Crafts' alkylation reaction between benzene and methyl chloride in presence of ferric chloride catalyst. With 30psia chloromethane pressure, the reaction proceeded to 100% conversion and the first two membranes provided a permeate of 99% pure toluene product. The third membrane was catalytically active to convert p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol in presence of sodium borohydride in ethanol. The purity of the permeate stream reached 98% in 40 min and was stable for 2 h. Thus, novel membranes were synthesized for enhanced separations.
texts.text: Thin films of oligomers were synthesized on PTFE substrates and they were studied for separation of product from liquid chemical reaction mixture. Three different types of membranes were fabricated using a technique called 'covalent molecular assembly'. Covalent molecular assembly is a synthesis technique in which a substrate is sequentially immersed in chemical solutions to grow molecular moieties with covalent bonding to the underlying layer of molecules on the substrate. The first membrane was oligoimide PMDA-ODA. The second membrane was crosslinked oligoamide TC-TAB. The third membrane was Nickel terminated oligoimide Ni-PMDA-ODA. The first two membranes were studied for Friedel-Crafts' alkylation reaction between benzene and methyl chloride in presence of ferric chloride catalyst. With 30psia chloromethane pressure, the reaction proceeded to 100% conversion and the first two membranes provided a permeate of 99% pure toluene product. The third membrane was catalytically active to convert p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol in presence of sodium borohydride in ethanol. The purity of the permeate stream reached 98% in 40 min and was stable for 2 h. Thus, novel membranes were synthesized for enhanced separations.

texts.orig: membrane synthesis technique employed was 'covalent molecular assembly' in which a substrate of macroporous PTFE was immersed in various solutions to grow molecular moieties with covalent bonds to the underlying layer. In literature, covalent molecular assembly was reported by Srinivasan et.al. [1-4]. Thin films of oligomers were synthesized on PTFE substrates and they were studied for separation of product from liquid chemical reaction mixture.

texts.orig: Types of membrane reactors are polymeric inert reactors, polymeric catalytic reactors, glass reactors, metallic reactors, dense ceramic reactors, porous ceramic reactors and zeolite reactors. A possible classification of polymeric membrane reactors is based on the role of the membrane in the chemical reaction process:
texts.text: Types of membrane reactors are polymeric inert reactors, polymeric catalytic reactors, glass reactors, metallic reactors, dense ceramic reactors, porous ceramic reactors and zeolite reactors. A possible classification of polymeric membrane reactors is based on the role of the membrane in the chemical reaction process:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Polymeric inert membrane reactors (PIMR), if the membrane provides only a separation function.
texts.text:  Polymeric inert membrane reactors (PIMR), if the membrane provides only a separation function.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Polymeric catalytic membrane reactors(PCMR), if the membrane is catalytically active.
texts.text:  Polymeric catalytic membrane reactors(PCMR), if the membrane is catalytically active.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PIMR are versatile for pervaporation use. Zhu et.al. [5] reported esterification of acetic acid and ethanol in a tube-and-shell type reactor, using a polymer ceramic composite membrane obtained by dip coating a ceramic support tube with a hydrophilic polyetherimide. Reactants, along with sulphuric acid as homogeneous catalyst were fed in the tube side while vacuum was applied to the shell side to collect water and ethyl acetate product. 75-82% conversion was obtained.
texts.text: PIMR are versatile for pervaporation use. Zhu et.al. [5] reported esterification of acetic acid and ethanol in a tube-and-shell type reactor, using a polymer ceramic composite membrane obtained by dip coating a ceramic support tube with a hydrophilic polyetherimide. Reactants, along with sulphuric acid as homogeneous catalyst were fed in the tube side while vacuum was applied to the shell side to collect water and ethyl acetate product. 75-82% conversion was obtained.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PCMR can be used to enhance chemical reactions and separate product. Porous composite polyethersulfone/poly(styrene sulfonate) hollow fibers containing palladium nanoparticles have been efficiently used in the liquid phase reduction of pnitrophenol to p-aminophenol by sodium borohydride in a forced flow reactor. The cross flow configuration and dead-end flow configuration were compared [6,7]. 80-95% reduction was obtained. J. Macanas et.al.[6] achieved about 95% conversion of p-nitrophenol in dead-end mode and tangential mode membrane filtration using metal nanoparticles loaded into microfiltration or ultrafiltration polymeric hollow fibers.
texts.text: PCMR can be used to enhance chemical reactions and separate product. Porous composite polyethersulfone/poly(styrene sulfonate) hollow fibers containing palladium nanoparticles have been efficiently used in the liquid phase reduction of pnitrophenol to p-aminophenol by sodium borohydride in a forced flow reactor. The cross flow configuration and dead-end flow configuration were compared [6,7]. 80-95% reduction was obtained. J. Macanas et.al.[6] achieved about 95% conversion of p-nitrophenol in dead-end mode and tangential mode membrane filtration using metal nanoparticles loaded into microfiltration or ultrafiltration polymeric hollow fibers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Kapton is a polyimide developed by DuPont, which can remain stable over a wide range of temperature � 273 to + 400 ° C. It is used in flexible printed circuits and micrometeroid garments, the outer layer of space-suits. Kapton is poly(4,4 0 -oxydiphenylene pyromellitimide). It is prepared from pyromellitic dianhydride and 4,4 0 -oxydianiline. It is a step polymerization reaction. The intermediate polyamic acid is soluble due to strong hydrogen bonds in the polar solvent used for the reaction. Thermal imidization ring-closure takes place at 200-300 ° C.
texts.text: Kapton is a polyimide developed by DuPont, which can remain stable over a wide range of temperature � 273 to + 400 ° C. It is used in flexible printed circuits and micrometeroid garments, the outer layer of space-suits. Kapton is poly(4,4 0 -oxydiphenylene pyromellitimide). It is prepared from pyromellitic dianhydride and 4,4 0 -oxydianiline. It is a step polymerization reaction. The intermediate polyamic acid is soluble due to strong hydrogen bonds in the polar solvent used for the reaction. Thermal imidization ring-closure takes place at 200-300 ° C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: UiO-66 is an archetypal metal � organic framework (MOF) with a very high surface area as well as high thermal stability. In reference [8], novel UiO-66-urea based flexible membranes were synthesized. Three membranes were prepared with various MOF loading - 50% (1), 60%(2) and 70%(3). The adsorption behavior of membrane 3 towards four hydrophilic dyes, namely, eosin Y (EY), rhodamine B (RB), malachite green (MG), and methylene blue (MB), in aqueous solution was studied in detail. It exhibits strong adsorption of EY and RB but weak adsorption of MG and MB in aqueous solution. Owing to the selective adsorption of these hydrophilic dyes, membrane 3 was able to remove EY and RB from aqueous solution and completely separate EY/MB, RB/MG, and RB/ MBmixtures in aqueous solution. Thus, these membranes could be used for dye adsorption and separation.
texts.text: UiO-66 is an archetypal metal � organic framework (MOF) with a very high surface area as well as high thermal stability. In reference [8], novel UiO-66-urea based flexible membranes were synthesized. Three membranes were prepared with various MOF loading - 50% (1), 60%(2) and 70%(3). The adsorption behavior of membrane 3 towards four hydrophilic dyes, namely, eosin Y (EY), rhodamine B (RB), malachite green (MG), and methylene blue (MB), in aqueous solution was studied in detail. It exhibits strong adsorption of EY and RB but weak adsorption of MG and MB in aqueous solution. Owing to the selective adsorption of these hydrophilic dyes, membrane 3 was able to remove EY and RB from aqueous solution and completely separate EY/MB, RB/MG, and RB/ MBmixtures in aqueous solution. Thus, these membranes could be used for dye adsorption and separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In reference [10], a hydrophobic highly stable POSS (polyhedral oligomeric silesquioxane) fabric membrane was prepared and it could separate oil/water mixture with ultrafast oil permeation flux. A membrane process can also concentrate a product like butanol from a dilute aqueous solution called fermentation broth as it is produced from a fermentation process [11].
texts.text: In reference [10], a hydrophobic highly stable POSS (polyhedral oligomeric silesquioxane) fabric membrane was prepared and it could separate oil/water mixture with ultrafast oil permeation flux. A membrane process can also concentrate a product like butanol from a dilute aqueous solution called fermentation broth as it is produced from a fermentation process [11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PTFE membrane substrates (PTU509010-90 mm diameter, 5micron pore size) were purchased from Sterlitech, Inc. All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Australia and Gelest, Inc., USA. Three different types of membranes were fabricated using a technique called 'covalent molecular assembly'. The first membrane was oligoimide PMDA-ODA. The second membrane was crosslinked oligoamide TC-TAB. The third membrane was Nickel terminated oligoimide Ni-PMDA-ODA.
texts.text: PTFE membrane substrates (PTU509010-90 mm diameter, 5micron pore size) were purchased from Sterlitech, Inc. All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Australia and Gelest, Inc., USA. Three different types of membranes were fabricated using a technique called 'covalent molecular assembly'. The first membrane was oligoimide PMDA-ODA. The second membrane was crosslinked oligoamide TC-TAB. The third membrane was Nickel terminated oligoimide Ni-PMDA-ODA.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: with 30 ml hydrogen peroxide. Slowly added drops of 30 ml hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to 70 ml of sulphuric acid(H2SO4) using droppers. Waited for colourless solution to cool. Mixed. Immersed 3 commercial PTFE discs into this piranha solution and left for 30 min. Removed the substrates from the solution using clean steel tweezers carefully and placed them on glass petri dishes. Wiped with tissues or blow dried with nitrogen. The PTFE discs now have � OH groups on their surfaces. At the end, carefully disposed the Piranha solution.

Immersed 3 commercial PTFE discs into this piranha solution and left for 30 min. Removed the substrates from the solution using clean steel tweezers carefully and placed them on glass petri dishes. Wiped with tissues or blow dried with nitrogen. The PTFE discs now have � OH groups on their surfaces. At the end, carefully disposed the Piranha solution.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1.2. Substrate modification
texts.text: 2.1.2. Substrate modification

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The 3 discs of PTFE substrates are on the benchtop. Took 4 clean glass jars (500 ml). In the first jar, filled 5% solution of 3(amino propyl) triethoxysilane in DiMethylAcetamide (5gm APtS in 95gm DMAc). Then, the three substrates were immersed into this APtS solution and left for 30 min. Ethanol comes off as reaction product. The substrate discs now have free amine groups on their surface. Meanwhile, solutions of PMDA and ODA were prepared. After 30 min, the substrates were removed from APtS solution with clean steel tweezers and placed on clean glass petri dishes. The 3 films were wiped with tissues to remove excess liquid from their surfaces. The APtS solution was carefully disposed in a separate waste container with label.
texts.text: The 3 discs of PTFE substrates are on the benchtop. Took 4 clean glass jars (500 ml). In the first jar, filled 5% solution of 3(amino propyl) triethoxysilane in DiMethylAcetamide (5gm APtS in 95gm DMAc). Then, the three substrates were immersed into this APtS solution and left for 30 min. Ethanol comes off as reaction product. The substrate discs now have free amine groups on their surface. Meanwhile, solutions of PMDA and ODA were prepared. After 30 min, the substrates were removed from APtS solution with clean steel tweezers and placed on clean glass petri dishes. The 3 films were wiped with tissues to remove excess liquid from their surfaces. The APtS solution was carefully disposed in a separate waste container with label.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1.3. Covalent molecular assembly process
texts.text: 2.1.3. Covalent molecular assembly process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the second glass jar, filled 5% solution of Pyromellitic dianhydride in DMAc(10gm PMDA in 190gm DMAc). In the third glass jar, filled 10% solution of Diaminodiphenyl ether in DMAc(10gm DDE in 90gm DMAc). The two solutions were placed on two separate stirring machines and they were dissolved using magnetic stirring. It took about 50 min for both solutions to become fully dissolved. Both PMDA and DDE solutions were golden yellow in colour. The PMDA solution was split into two halves to prevent scum formation. Next, the 3 substrates were immersed into the PMDA solution using tweezers and left for 30 min. The substrates now have polya- mic acid containing anhydride groups. The films were wiped with tissues to remove excess liquid. Next, the 3 substrates were immersed into the DDE solution using tweezers and left for 30 min. The substrates now have ether containing free amine group. The films were alternately immersed into the PMDA and DDE solutions with intermittent wiping with tissues. One film was kept aside after 4 layers completed. The remaining two films were immersed for total 8 layers. Total time (8x30minutes = 240 min = 4 h). Then, the films were placed on glass petri dishes. The substrates now had grown chains of polyamic acid.
texts.text: In the second glass jar, filled 5% solution of Pyromellitic dianhydride in DMAc(10gm PMDA in 190gm DMAc). In the third glass jar, filled 10% solution of Diaminodiphenyl ether in DMAc(10gm DDE in 90gm DMAc). The two solutions were placed on two separate stirring machines and they were dissolved using magnetic stirring. It took about 50 min for both solutions to become fully dissolved. Both PMDA and DDE solutions were golden yellow in colour. The PMDA solution was split into two halves to prevent scum formation. Next, the 3 substrates were immersed into the PMDA solution using tweezers and left for 30 min. The substrates now have polya- mic acid containing anhydride groups. The films were wiped with tissues to remove excess liquid. Next, the 3 substrates were immersed into the DDE solution using tweezers and left for 30 min. The substrates now have ether containing free amine group. The films were alternately immersed into the PMDA and DDE solutions with intermittent wiping with tissues. One film was kept aside after 4 layers completed. The remaining two films were immersed for total 8 layers. Total time (8x30minutes = 240 min = 4 h). Then, the films were placed on glass petri dishes. The substrates now had grown chains of polyamic acid.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Placed the glass petri dishes with the 3 substrates inside an oven. Numbered the films. Turned on oven and set to 150 ° C for 18 h. Placing the films in pre-heated oven also gives same results. This step causes ring-closure and formation of imide bonds to result in poly(4,4 0 -oxydiphenylene pyromellitimide) membrane films. This process also causes entanglement of chains to yield a dense membrane of average pore size. This process also causes evaporation of residual solvent and drying. The next day, turned off the oven.
texts.text: Placed the glass petri dishes with the 3 substrates inside an oven. Numbered the films. Turned on oven and set to 150 ° C for 18 h. Placing the films in pre-heated oven also gives same results. This step causes ring-closure and formation of imide bonds to result in poly(4,4 0 -oxydiphenylene pyromellitimide) membrane films. This process also causes entanglement of chains to yield a dense membrane of average pore size. This process also causes evaporation of residual solvent and drying. The next day, turned off the oven.

texts.orig: Removed the membrane films and stored in Ziplock bags. Sealed the bags and labelled name of membranes/ date/10 steps. The membrane films were white or light yellow in colour. Thickness of the membrane films were 200-240 l m (0.20-0.24 mm). Fig. 1 shows the schematic diagram of the oligoimide membrane 1 films. The density of PMDA-ODA membrane films was 1.1318gm/cc.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.2.2. Surface modification: Prepared 5% solution of aminophenyl trimethoxy silane (APhS) in DMAc and immersed the 4 PTFE substrates in it for 30 min.
texts.text: 2.2.2. Surface modification: Prepared 5% solution of aminophenyl trimethoxy silane (APhS) in DMAc and immersed the 4 PTFE substrates in it for 30 min.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.2.3. Covalent molecular assembly: 10% solution of terepthaloyl chloride was prepared by dissolving 10gm TC in 90 gm DMAc. Two such solutions were prepared in a time interval of 1 h 30 min to prevent scum formation. TC gets dissolved in DMAc by minimal stirring and TC solutions were colourless. 2.5% solution of 1,2,4,5tetraaminobenzene was prepared by dissolving 2.5gm of TAB in 97.5gm of solvent DMAc. Stirring machine was used for mixing. The TAB solution was turquoise blue in colour. The four PTFE films were immersed into the TC solution using tweezers and left for 30 min. The films now have free chloride groups on their surface. Then, the PTFE films were wiped with tissues and immersed into the TAB solution for 30 min. This causes intentional crosslinking of oligomeric chains on the PTFE substrates. HCL comes off as reaction product and amide groups get formed. The surface is now rich in crosslinked and free amine groups from TAB. The PTFE substrates were alternately immersed in the TC and TAB solutions for 15-30 min each. One film was kept aside after 4 layers of TC and TAB (6 deposition steps). The remaining three films underwent 8 layers of TC and TAB immersion (10 deposition steps).
texts.text: 2.2.3. Covalent molecular assembly: 10% solution of terepthaloyl chloride was prepared by dissolving 10gm TC in 90 gm DMAc. Two such solutions were prepared in a time interval of 1 h 30 min to prevent scum formation. TC gets dissolved in DMAc by minimal stirring and TC solutions were colourless. 2.5% solution of 1,2,4,5tetraaminobenzene was prepared by dissolving 2.5gm of TAB in 97.5gm of solvent DMAc. Stirring machine was used for mixing. The TAB solution was turquoise blue in colour. The four PTFE films were immersed into the TC solution using tweezers and left for 30 min. The films now have free chloride groups on their surface. Then, the PTFE films were wiped with tissues and immersed into the TAB solution for 30 min. This causes intentional crosslinking of oligomeric chains on the PTFE substrates. HCL comes off as reaction product and amide groups get formed. The surface is now rich in crosslinked and free amine groups from TAB. The PTFE substrates were alternately immersed in the TC and TAB solutions for 15-30 min each. One film was kept aside after 4 layers of TC and TAB (6 deposition steps). The remaining three films underwent 8 layers of TC and TAB immersion (10 deposition steps).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.2.4. Drying: A furnace was pre-heated to 150 ° C and programmed to maintain for 24 h. The four PTFE films were numbered and placed inside the oven. The films were at 150 ° C for 16 h. This step causes evaporation of solvent and other liquids in the films. This step also causes densification to yield a membrane of average pore size. The next day, the oven was turned off and let cool. The films were purple in colour. The films were 0.21-0.23 mm in thickness. The films were stored in labelled ziplock bags. The chemical molecular structure of the covalent molecular assembly for this second type of membrane is shown in Fig. 2. Its nomenclature is
texts.text: 2.2.4. Drying: A furnace was pre-heated to 150 ° C and programmed to maintain for 24 h. The four PTFE films were numbered and placed inside the oven. The films were at 150 ° C for 16 h. This step causes evaporation of solvent and other liquids in the films. This step also causes densification to yield a membrane of average pore size. The next day, the oven was turned off and let cool. The films were purple in colour. The films were 0.21-0.23 mm in thickness. The films were stored in labelled ziplock bags. The chemical molecular structure of the covalent molecular assembly for this second type of membrane is shown in Fig. 2. Its nomenclature is

texts.orig: 1,2,4,5-tetramido 4,4 0 terepthaloyl diamide. The density of TCTAB membrane films was 1.3443gm/cc.

texts.orig: 2.3. Fabrication of Nickel terminated PMDA-ODA membrane films
texts.text: 2.3. Fabrication of Nickel terminated PMDA-ODA membrane films

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The procedure was same as in section 2.1.1 till 2.1.3. After that, the 4 PTFE substrates were immersed in a 5% solution of Nickel acetate in water for 30 min. Then, the films were thermally imidized and dried in an oven at 150 ° C for 16 h. The chemical structure of this novel oligomeric Ni-PMDA-ODA membrane is shown in Fig. 3. The films were 0.19 mm-0.22 mm in thickness. The density of Nickel-PMDA-ODA membrane films was 1.3519gm/cc.
texts.text: The procedure was same as in section 2.1.1 till 2.1.3. After that, the 4 PTFE substrates were immersed in a 5% solution of Nickel acetate in water for 30 min. Then, the films were thermally imidized and dried in an oven at 150 ° C for 16 h. The chemical structure of this novel oligomeric Ni-PMDA-ODA membrane is shown in Fig. 3. The films were 0.19 mm-0.22 mm in thickness. The density of Nickel-PMDA-ODA membrane films was 1.3519gm/cc.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Experimental results of simultaneous chemical reaction and membrane separation:
texts.text: 4. Experimental results of simultaneous chemical reaction and membrane separation:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A membrane permeation system was constructed using HP4750 stirred cell from Sterlitech TM and Swagelok Ò parts. The experimental setup is shown in Fig. 5. The oligomeric membranes fabricated as in section 2 were mounted inside the 300 ml cell. The cell was filled with a mixture of chemical reaction solution comprising 180 ml benzene(158gm), 5gm ferric chloride and methyl chloride gas. A stir bar was inserted into the cell. The cell was kept on top of a stirring machine. The cell was closed with tight clamps. The top of the cell was connected to a nitrogen gas cylinder via a 3way valve. N2 size E gas cylinders (034E) were purchased from BOC gases, AU and strapped below fume hood. Methyl chloride gas was injected into the solution in the cell. The chemical reaction proceeds. Feed pressure in the range 30psi to 100psi was applied through the nitrogen gas which serves as driving force for membrane permeation process. The permeate starts flowing out and is collected downstream in a glass beaker. Within about 3 h, the reaction is complete. The permeate is analyzed in HPLC. The remaining solution inside the cell is called reject and is analyzed by HPLC.
texts.text: A membrane permeation system was constructed using HP4750 stirred cell from Sterlitech TM and Swagelok Ò parts. The experimental setup is shown in Fig. 5. The oligomeric membranes fabricated as in section 2 were mounted inside the 300 ml cell. The cell was filled with a mixture of chemical reaction solution comprising 180 ml benzene(158gm), 5gm ferric chloride and methyl chloride gas. A stir bar was inserted into the cell. The cell was kept on top of a stirring machine. The cell was closed with tight clamps. The top of the cell was connected to a nitrogen gas cylinder via a 3way valve. N2 size E gas cylinders (034E) were purchased from BOC gases, AU and strapped below fume hood. Methyl chloride gas was injected into the solution in the cell. The chemical reaction proceeds. Feed pressure in the range 30psi to 100psi was applied through the nitrogen gas which serves as driving force for membrane permeation process. The permeate starts flowing out and is collected downstream in a glass beaker. Within about 3 h, the reaction is complete. The permeate is analyzed in HPLC. The remaining solution inside the cell is called reject and is analyzed by HPLC.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first two membranes were studied for Friedel-Crafts' alkylation reaction between benzene (5.3-5.8 Å) and methyl chloride in presence of ferric chloride catalyst. Toluene (5.25 Å) is the primary product while xylene isomers (o-xylene = 6.8 Å, m-xylene = 6.8 Å, p-xylene = 5.8 Å) are side-products of over-alkylation. In the first set of experiments, 10psia of chloromethane was applied but the results were poor. In the second set of experiments, higher pressure 30psia of chloromethane was applied for longer time to ensure that the chemical reaction occurs properly. The permeate and reject streams were analysed by HPLC (high pressure liquid chromatography). Multiple experiments were performed. About 90 sample vials were collected/prepared and analysed. The HPLC
texts.text: The first two membranes were studied for Friedel-Crafts' alkylation reaction between benzene (5.3-5.8 Å) and methyl chloride in presence of ferric chloride catalyst. Toluene (5.25 Å) is the primary product while xylene isomers (o-xylene = 6.8 Å, m-xylene = 6.8 Å, p-xylene = 5.8 Å) are side-products of over-alkylation. In the first set of experiments, 10psia of chloromethane was applied but the results were poor. In the second set of experiments, higher pressure 30psia of chloromethane was applied for longer time to ensure that the chemical reaction occurs properly. The permeate and reject streams were analysed by HPLC (high pressure liquid chromatography). Multiple experiments were performed. About 90 sample vials were collected/prepared and analysed. The HPLC

texts.orig: ran for many days. 30psia of Chloromethane gas was injected for 5 min at regular intervals over 3 h for making the chemical reaction occur to complete conversion. Only one product was formed that was hypothesized to be toluene.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Maintaining 80psia N2 pressure, the PMDA-ODA membrane provided a permeate of 99.7% pure toluene product with traces of benzene. The permeate flow rate was 0.322-0.5 ml/min. Reject was 100% pure product toluene with traces of dissolved ferric chloride. These results were stable for 3 h. Permeability = 1.62x10 -8 ml : cm sec : cm 2 : cmHg .
texts.text: Maintaining 80psia N2 pressure, the PMDA-ODA membrane provided a permeate of 99.7% pure toluene product with traces of benzene. The permeate flow rate was 0.322-0.5 ml/min. Reject was 100% pure product toluene with traces of dissolved ferric chloride. These results were stable for 3 h. Permeability = 1.62x10 -8 ml : cm sec : cm 2 : cmHg .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Maintaining 80psia N2 pressure, the TC-TAB crosslinked membrane provided a permeate with 99.8%toluene with traces of benzene. The permeate flow rate was 0.289-0.42 ml/min. Reject was 99.3% pure product toluene with traces of dissolved ferric chloride. These results were stable for 3 h. Permeability = 1.526x10 8 ml : cm sec : cm 2 : cmHg .
texts.text: Maintaining 80psia N2 pressure, the TC-TAB crosslinked membrane provided a permeate with 99.8%toluene with traces of benzene. The permeate flow rate was 0.289-0.42 ml/min. Reject was 99.3% pure product toluene with traces of dissolved ferric chloride. These results were stable for 3 h. Permeability = 1.526x10 8 ml : cm sec : cm 2 : cmHg .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The chemical reaction occurred to 100% conversion and membrane separation played a significant role. The membranes were important for the reactor configuration required to get 100% conversion. The feed solution was black in colour. The colour of the reject remaining inside the reactor cell was orange when 30psia CH3Cl was applied as opposed to dark brown when only 10psia CH3Cl was applied. Permeate were yellow in colour. These experiments were repeated with more membranes and same results were obtained.

texts.orig: Fig. 6 shows the percentage purity of product toluene in the permeate stream which was 99% over three hours' time period when PMDA-ODA membranes was used. A similar results' graph was obtained for TC-TAB membrane.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 7. Variation of p-aminophenol product purity with time in permeate stream from Nickel-PMDA-ODA membrane.
texts.text: Fig. 7. Variation of p-aminophenol product purity with time in permeate stream from Nickel-PMDA-ODA membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The third membrane Nickel-PMDA-ODA was catalytically active to convert p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol in presence of sodium borohydride in ethanol. The catalyst was the Nickel bonded to the surface of the membrane. The feed solution was 180 ml ethanol, 1gm NaBH4, 5gm p-nitrophenol. The chloromethane valve was kept closed. 50-80psia nitrogen pressure was applied. As the feed solution contacted the membrane, it got converted to paminophenol. Under pressure, the product p-aminophenol permeated through the membrane. The purity of the permeate stream for p-aminophenol gradually increased with time and reached 98.18% in 40 min and was stable for 2 h. It is shown in Fig. 7. The permeate flow rate was 0.275-0.5 ml/min. Yield=(actual yield/theoretical yield)x100 = 27.8%. Conversion = 45.3%.
texts.text: The third membrane Nickel-PMDA-ODA was catalytically active to convert p-nitrophenol to p-aminophenol in presence of sodium borohydride in ethanol. The catalyst was the Nickel bonded to the surface of the membrane. The feed solution was 180 ml ethanol, 1gm NaBH4, 5gm p-nitrophenol. The chloromethane valve was kept closed. 50-80psia nitrogen pressure was applied. As the feed solution contacted the membrane, it got converted to paminophenol. Under pressure, the product p-aminophenol permeated through the membrane. The purity of the permeate stream for p-aminophenol gradually increased with time and reached 98.18% in 40 min and was stable for 2 h. It is shown in Fig. 7. The permeate flow rate was 0.275-0.5 ml/min. Yield=(actual yield/theoretical yield)x100 = 27.8%. Conversion = 45.3%.

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Permeability = 5.117x10 -8 ml : cm sec : cm 2 : cmHg

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Upon repeating the experiments with few more Nickel-PMDAODA membranes, the permeate flow rate was higher at 2.96 ml/ min and permeate recovery reached 82.2% in 1 h. This membrane had more pores. Yield was 81.7%. Conversion was 93.9%. The permeate purity for p-aminophenol reached 99.4% in 8 min and was stable for 1 h. Reproducible permeate purity was obtained. Permeability = 2.892x10 -7 ml : cm sec : cm 2 : cmHg .
texts.text: Upon repeating the experiments with few more Nickel-PMDAODA membranes, the permeate flow rate was higher at 2.96 ml/ min and permeate recovery reached 82.2% in 1 h. This membrane had more pores. Yield was 81.7%. Conversion was 93.9%. The permeate purity for p-aminophenol reached 99.4% in 8 min and was stable for 1 h. Reproducible permeate purity was obtained. Permeability = 2.892x10 -7 ml : cm sec : cm 2 : cmHg .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Novel oligomeric membranes were synthesized by covalent molecular assembly technique for enhanced separations. Commercially available macroporous PTFE substrates were subject to surface modification and covalent molecular assembly to yield dense microporous membrane films that aided to obtain 100%conversion in liquid chemical reactions and also provide separation of product. The PMDA-ODA membrane and TC-TAB membranes, both, provided 100% conversion of benzene to 99% pure toluene product. The Nickel terminated PMDA-ODA membrane catalytically converted p-nitrophenol to 98-99%p-aminophenol product in 840 min.
texts.text: Novel oligomeric membranes were synthesized by covalent molecular assembly technique for enhanced separations. Commercially available macroporous PTFE substrates were subject to surface modification and covalent molecular assembly to yield dense microporous membrane films that aided to obtain 100%conversion in liquid chemical reactions and also provide separation of product. The PMDA-ODA membrane and TC-TAB membranes, both, provided 100% conversion of benzene to 99% pure toluene product. The Nickel terminated PMDA-ODA membrane catalytically converted p-nitrophenol to 98-99%p-aminophenol product in 840 min.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane reactors can enhance conversion in chemical reaction processes and lower cost. With increasing awareness about membranes to make industrial processes (small, medium) more efficient and more economical, there is potential for researchers to convert membrane reactors from thin films to tubular form and employ them for real-time processes. Some challenges remain such as membrane fabrication for large scale processes, accuracy and reproducibility of fabrication techniques and sealing the membranes into modules for large scale processes.
texts.text: Membrane reactors can enhance conversion in chemical reaction processes and lower cost. With increasing awareness about membranes to make industrial processes (small, medium) more efficient and more economical, there is potential for researchers to convert membrane reactors from thin films to tubular form and employ them for real-time processes. Some challenges remain such as membrane fabrication for large scale processes, accuracy and reproducibility of fabrication techniques and sealing the membranes into modules for large scale processes.
